title: Ribollita

overview.description: A hearty Tuscan bread and vegetable soup thickened with day-old bread and cannellini beans. Traditionally made with leftover vegetables and bread, it's a comforting, economical meal that's even better the following day.

ingredients: 200g day-old rustic bread, torn into pieces
Salt and freshly ground black pepper
Extra virgin olive oil, to serve
method: Heat the olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Cook the onion, carrot and celery for about 10 minutes until softened.
Add the garlic, tomato purée, thyme and rosemary. Cook for 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Stir in the cannellini beans, chopped tomatoes and stock, then simmer gently for 20 minutes.
Add the kale and cook for another 5 minutes until tender.
Lightly mash some of the beans against the side of the pan to naturally thicken the soup.
Stir in the torn bread and cook for a further 5 minutes until it breaks down and thickens the soup.
Season with salt and black pepper to taste.
Serve with grated Parmesan (if using) and a generous drizzle of extra virgin olive oil.

learning.did-you-know: Ribollita means 'reboiled'. Tuscan families traditionally reheated the soup over several days, and many believe it tastes even better after resting overnight.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How stale bread can be used to naturally thicken soup, how to build a traditional Italian soffritto, and how humble ingredients create rich flavour.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A perfect Pantry Monday meal that celebrates reducing food waste while producing a filling, nutritious family dinner.
chapman-tips: Day-old bread works much better than fresh bread.
Don't blend the soup—the rustic texture is part of its character.
A good quality olive oil added at the table makes a big difference.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well, although the bread softens further. Alternatively, freeze before adding the bread and stir fresh bread in when reheating.
notes.leftovers: Often tastes even better the following day. Add a splash of stock when reheating if it has become too thick.
